sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 10, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 9 of about 39 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The player didn't move further down - it's still at rows 44-47. This means the player stopped at rows 44-47 and couldn't move further down. The yellow blocks start at row 52, so there are rows 48-51 between them (4 rows of orange). The player is 4 rows tall and was at 44-47, so moving DOWN by 4 would put it at 48-51, and another DOWN would put it at 52-55 which overlaps with the yellow blocks.
Wait, but the player is still at rows 44-47! That means the DOWN actions didn't move it. Let me re-check... Actually, looking at the output, the player boundary is still [[44, 12], [44, 31], [47, 31], [47, 12]]. But I executed 2 DOWN actions. 
Hmm, wait - maybe the player DID move but the display shows it at the same position because of how the boundary works. Let me look more carefully. The previous state showed the player at rows 44-47, and now it's still at 44-47. So the DOWN actions didn't move the player.
But wait - the timer went from 49 to 45 pixels (N: boundary=[[0, 0], [0, 44]], pixels=45). That's a decrease of 4 pixels for 2 actions, which is consistent with -2 per action.
So the player didn't move further down. Maybe there's an obstacle or the player can only move a limited distance. Let me check if there's something between the player and the yellow blocks. Looking at the ascii, rows 48-51 are all orange (O), so there's no visible obstacle.
Wait, maybe the player stopped because it hit something. Let me look at the boundary more carefully. The player is at rows 44-47, cols 12-31. The yellow block on the left starts at row 52, cols 16-27. There's a 4-row gap (rows 48-51) between them.
Actually, I think the issue might be that the player CAN move down but there's some collision or constraint. Let me try moving LEFT or RIGHT instead to see if horizontal movement works, and also try to understand the game better.
Actually wait - maybe I should reconsider. The player was at rows 44-47 before the 2 DOWN actions, and it's still at 44-47. But the timer decreased. So the actions were registered but the player didn't move. Maybe there's a collision with something I'm not seeing, or maybe the player can only move a certain number of times.
Let me try moving LEFT or RIGHT to see if horizontal movement works.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player stuck at rows 44-47 - DOWN didn't work (maybe collision or limit)
# Timer: 45 pixels (was 49, decreased by 4 for 2 actions)
# Let me try LEFT and RIGHT to test horizontal movement

action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"DOWN": "Moves blue player 4 rows down, decreases timer by 2 pixels VERIFIED(step 2); may have movement limit or collision"}, "object_taxonomy": {"N_strip_top": "Green timer bar at row 0, shrinks by 2 per action", "O_background": "Orange main background", "c_block": "Charcoal block at rows 1-3, cols 36-39", "M_block": "Magenta block at rows 4-7, cols 36-39", "b_rectangle": "Blue 4x20 player rectangle, moves with arrow keys", "Y_shapes": "Two U-shaped yellow containers at bottom rows 52-59", "w_strip_bottom": "Light gray floor rows 60-63"}, "hud_map": {"top_green_strip": "Timer/progress bar"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Blue 4x20 rectangle is player at rows 44-47, cols 12-31. Player stopped moving down - possible collision or limit. Two U-shaped yellow containers at bottom rows 52-59. Charcoal+magenta blocks near top.", "goal_model": "Unknown - need to explore more movement", "recent_findings": ["Player stopped at rows 44-47, DOWN no longer moves it", "Timer still decreases even when player doesn't move"], "open_questions": ["Why did player stop moving down?", "Does LEFT/RIGHT work?", "What is the goal?"], "current_plan": "Test horizontal movement with LEFT", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
